problem_summary: 2023 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations research / network science). The task: build a network of the 17 UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) showing their relationships; design a priority-evaluation system to rank which SDGs the UN should pursue first and forecast 10-year impact after prioritization; analyze how achieving one SDG or adding a new SDG reshapes the network and priorities; assess how global crises (pandemics, wars, climate disasters, refugee movements, tech advances) perturb the network; and generalize the approach so any organization can prioritize its own goals. This team's answer is "DIGNP" (Dynamic Indicator-Goal Network for Prioritization): a two-layer weighted directed graph (indicator layer mapped up to a goal layer) driven by a System Dynamics framing, scored by five graph-centrality priority metrics fused via Entropy Weight Method, and projected forward with ARIMA augmented by a coupling "priority factor."
models_used: Two-layer weighted directed network: an Indicator Network (44 selected indicator nodes, fully connected) mapped up to a 17-node Goal Network (SDG Network), framed under System Dynamics modeling

Pearson correlation coefficient as the edge-weight primitive: used twice — (a) to rank/select the most influential 2-4 indicators per SDG via total absolute correlation r_i,total = sum|r_ij| (cut 241 official indicators down to 44), and (b) to set indicator-to-indicator edge weights (C(44,2)=946 coefficients)

Coefficient of Variation Method (CVM) to weight each indicator's contribution lambda(I_n(j)) by its variation relative to target, then aggregate indicator-level correlations up into Goal-to-Goal edge weights (Inact_{Gm->Gn})

Five graph-theoretic priority indicators on the Goal Network: (1) Node Efficiency (NE), (2) Weighted Eigenvector Centrality (WEC, custom-modified for a complete graph using largest eigenvalue of weighted adjacency matrix), (3) Betweenness Centrality (BC) computed via Dijkstra shortest paths with edge length = 1/weight, (4) Sum of Relative Influence Intensity (SRI), (5) Sum of Maximum Network Flow (SMNF) solved as a max-flow LP via Ford-Fulkerson
Entropy Weight Method (EWM) to objectively fuse the five priority indicators into a single Priority Score PS(G_i) = sum mu_k * indicator_k

ARIMA(p,d,q) time-series forecasting of each indicator (selected p=4, d=1, q=2), augmented with a multiplicative 'priority factor' PF_m(n) = PS(Gm)*PS(Gn)/[max_i PS(Gi)]^2 to inject inter-goal coupling into the forecast

Dynamic network update algorithms (Algorithm 1 for goal accomplishment/node subtraction; Algorithm 2 for goal inclusion/node addition) that zero-out or add edges, recompute goal weights, and re-rank priorities automatically
Linear/min-max normalization of indicators against optimal target values, with separate cost-minimized vs benefit-maximized formulas and an 'achieved'/'over-achieved' flag at x_nor <= 0
key_techniques: Two-layer abstraction with automatic upward mapping: build the graph at the granular indicator level (where real correlation data exists), then deterministically derive the goal-level graph from it. This turns a vague 'how are SDGs related?' question into a concrete computable pipeline.

Using total absolute correlation as a feature-selection filter to shrink 241 indicators to a tractable 44, with a fully worked SDG 7 example (heatmap + ranked table) so judges can audit the selection logic.

Five complementary centrality/flow metrics chosen so each captures a distinct, real-world-interpretable notion of 'priority' (reachability, neighbor importance, bridging, relative influence share, throughput) rather than one arbitrary measure — then letting EWM decide their weights objectively instead of guessing.

Reframing physical/causal phenomena as graph operations: 'goal achieved' = node deletion (edges -> 0), 'new goal' = node addition, 'crisis' = targeted edge-weight increase between specific SDG pairs. Every qualitative scenario becomes a mechanical, reproducible network edit.

Coupling the forecast to the network: ARIMA gives each indicator's organic trajectory, and the priority factor PF adds a portion of a prioritized goal's gain onto coupled goals, operationalizing the 'progress in one SDG drives others' premise quantitatively.

Concrete real-world validation anchors: comparing the priority ranking against the Copenhagen Consensus Center's SDG ordering; grounding the crisis case in the 2019-2020 Australian bushfires (18M hectares, ~830 Mt CO2e); back-testing ARIMA on 2000-2015 -> 2016-2020 hold-out.
Edge-length-as-inverse-weight trick so that standard shortest-path/Dijkstra and betweenness machinery work correctly on an influence graph (high influence = short distance).
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective this paper wins on completeness, mechanization, and self-consistency. It answers every sub-question of Problem D with the SAME machinery rather than bolting on disconnected models: the two-layer network handles 'relationships,' the five-metric+EWM stack handles 'priority,' node add/delete handles 'achieving/adding a goal,' edge-weight bumps handle 'crises,' and the whole thing generalizes verbatim into DIGNP with a six-step recipe and a mock UI. That structural unity is rare and reads as a genuine framework, not a checklist. The priority engine is also defensible: five distinct, individually-motivated graph metrics fused by an OBJECTIVE weighting method (EWM) sidesteps the common judge complaint that AHP/expert weights are arbitrary. The team validated against an external authority (Copenhagen Consensus ranking) and back-tested the forecaster, signaling intellectual honesty. Finally the operations-research toolbox is broad and correctly applied — Dijkstra, Ford-Fulkerson max-flow, eigenvector centrality, ARIMA, EWM, CVM — which demonstrates the modeling maturity O-papers are expected to show. The named, brandable framework (DIGNP) plus a clean problem-to-method flowchart gives judges an easy mental handle on the whole paper."
section_highlights: "Strong, modeler-friendly structure throughout. The Summary front-loads concrete results (top-5 priority = SDG 17, 3, 1, 4, 13) and the ICT-as-SDG-18 recommendation, so a judge knows the payoff in 30 seconds. Section 2 defines every object (Indicator/Goal node, Indicator/Goal Network, edge weight) and lists three justified assumptions plus a full notation table before any math — excellent rigor hygiene. The Indicator->Goal mapping is given its own 'innovative idea' callout (Fig 8) so the central insight is unmissable. The five priority metrics are each introduced with a plain-English real-world interpretation BEFORE the formula (e.g. NE = 'how much UN can achieve elsewhere while focusing on one SDG'), which is exactly the equation-then-meaning balance judges reward. Dynamic behavior is formalized as two clean pseudocode Algorithms (1 and 2) keyed to numbered equations. The crisis section uses one deep worked case (Australian bushfires) plus a tidy bulleted catalog of other crises mapped to specific SDG-pair correlation increases. The paper closes the loop with explicit generalization (DIGNP six steps), a UI mockup, sensitivity analysis on ARIMA's p (back-tested), and an honest Strength/Weakness section. Heavy, well-captioned use of heatmaps, network diagrams, and prediction line charts."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The data and several figures appear illustrative/fabricated rather than computed: the SDG 7 correlation heatmap has non-symmetric, suspiciously round values (0.85, 0.95...) for what should be a symmetric correlation matrix; the Fig 10 'before vs after prioritization' poverty curves are identical columns; many mermaid network diagrams are garbled or duplicated (e.g. Fig 12's runaway repeated 'W_ind=0' chain, Fig 17's dozens of duplicate style lines). A careful judge could doubt whether the pipeline was actually run end-to-end.

Treating Pearson correlation as a directed causal influence and as edge weight conflates correlation with causation — the System Dynamics 'feedback loop' framing is asserted but never actually implemented as stocks/flows/ODEs (it's static correlation, not true System Dynamics).

The network is a complete (fully connected) graph, which weakens several metrics: betweenness centrality is near-degenerate when everything is directly connected, and the team had to ad-hoc 'modify' eigenvector centrality to cope. Many topological measures lose discriminative power on a dense graph.
Edge weights derived from only Y=23 yearly points (often fewer per indicator) make correlation estimates noisy; selecting just 2-4 indicators per SDG (44 total of 241) discards most official signal.

The ARIMA + priority-factor forecast double-counts coupling (ARIMA already fits historical co-movement, then PF adds more cross-goal gain on top) and the PF formula is heuristic with no calibration. Forecast horizons to 2030 from sparse data are weakly justified.
EWM weights are computed from the same constructed (possibly synthetic) priority-indicator values, so 'objectivity' is only as good as the underlying data.
Assumption 2 ('nodes impact each other within months/a year') is convenient for a static-correlation model but unrealistic for many SDG linkages, undermining the dynamic-prediction credibility.
reusable_patterns: Two-layer network template (granular-feature layer -> aggregate-entity layer with an automatic mapping): broadly reusable whenever a problem asks to relate high-level entities that lack direct data but whose sub-components are measurable. Build edges where data lives, then project up.

Multi-metric + objective-fusion ranking stack: compute several independent, individually-interpretable scores (here 5 graph centralities), then combine with EWM or entropy weighting instead of arbitrary expert weights. A clean, defensible answer to ANY 'rank/prioritize these N things' OR problem.

Scenario-as-graph-edit pattern: encode every what-if (entity removed, entity added, external shock) as a node add/delete or targeted edge-weight change, then re-run the same scoring pipeline. Reusable for any dynamic-network or robustness sub-question.

Forecast-plus-coupling-term pattern: run a standard univariate forecaster (ARIMA) per series, then add an interaction/coupling term derived from the network to capture inter-series spillover. Generalizes to any 'predict outcomes after intervention' task.
Inverse-weight-as-distance trick to reuse shortest-path/betweenness/flow algorithms on an influence or similarity graph.

'Name your framework + flowchart + generalization recipe + UI mockup' packaging: brand the method (DIGNP), give a one-figure pipeline, a numbered generalization procedure, and a mock interface — a repeatable way to score the generalization/communication parts of any MCM problem.

Worked single-example before full results (the SDG 7 indicator-selection walkthrough with heatmap and ranked table), then 'full list in Appendix' — an efficient way to demonstrate method correctness without bloating the body.

External-benchmark validation habit: sanity-check your model's ranking against a recognized third-party ranking (Copenhagen Consensus) and back-test forecasts on a held-out period to choose hyperparameters.
